metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the AC2(d) oracle to account for Python's early-binding of default parameters: either (a) have the test call `prune(ticket_id, keep=module.RETAIN_PER_TICKET)` after monkeypatching so it reads the patched module attribute explicitly, (b) rewrite `prune` to read the constant at call time (e.g. `keep=None` with `if keep is None: keep = RETAIN_PER_TICKET` in the body) so monkeypatching is effective, or (c) replace the monkeypatch sub-check with a static assertion that `code_review.sidecar.prune.__kwdefaults__['keep']` is the same object as `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (complementing AC2(c)) and rely on AC2(b)'s source-scan for the 'no-copy' guarantee.
findings.finding: AC2(d)'s monkeypatch oracle is broken by Python's early-binding of default parameter values: `def prune(..., keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET)` captures the integer 50 at function-definition time; monkeypatching `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` after import does NOT change the already-captured `keep` default, so calling `prune(ticket_id)` (without an explicit `keep`) will always retain 50 regardless of the monkeypatch — the test can never demonstrate that 'both prune paths read the one constant at prune time, not a captured copy'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2, sub-clause (d)
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt approach (b): define both prune functions with `keep=None` and resolve `if keep is None: keep = RETAIN_PER_TICKET` inside the body. Then the monkeypatch test works correctly — patch `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 3`, call `prune(ticket_id)` on both paths, assert retained count == 3. Update AC2(d) accordingly and update the function signature in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between Scope's `keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET` default-parameter spec and AC #2d's monkeypatch oracle: either (a) change the prune signature to `keep=None` and resolve `if keep is None: keep = RETAIN_PER_TICKET` inside the body (so the constant is read at call time), or (b) rewrite AC #2d's oracle to not rely on monkeypatching the bound default (e.g., test identity via `prune.__kwdefaults__` or direct `keep` override calls).
findings.finding: AC #2d requires that monkeypatching the single constant causes BOTH prune functions to retain that new value, proving they 'read the one constant at prune time, not a captured copy' — but the Scope implementation spec says to define `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, ...)`, which in Python binds the default value ONCE at function-definition time (import time). Monkeypatching the module-level name afterward does NOT change an already-bound default argument, so the test oracle in AC #2d would fail with the implementation described in Scope.
findings.location: Scope (code_review/sidecar.py bullet: `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, ...)`) vs AC #2d ('monkeypatches the single constant to a small N and asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N — proving both paths read the one constant at prune time, not a captured copy')
findings.suggested_fix: Change the prune signature to `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=None, repo_root=None)` with `if keep is None: keep = RETAIN_PER_TICKET` as the first line of the body. This makes the function read the current module-level constant at every call, so monkeypatching `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` before calling `prune()` will be observed — satisfying AC #2d's oracle while preserving the single-constant semantics described in the Approach.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC2 oracle (b) to scope the 'exactly one RETAIN_PER_TICKET = assignment' source-scan to only the two affected modules (plan_review/sidecar.py and code_review/sidecar.py), or restate the invariant as 'code_review/sidecar.py contains no RETAIN_PER_TICKET = assignment' — because completion_sidecar.py and overlap/digest_sidecar.py already define independent same-named constants that would cause the broader scan to fail.
findings.finding: AC2 oracle sub-assertion (b) specifies a test that asserts 'there is exactly ONE RETAIN_PER_TICKET = assignment in src/rebar/', but the codebase already contains at least three independent RETAIN_PER_TICKET = assignments (plan_review/sidecar.py:43, completion_sidecar.py:34, overlap/digest_sidecar.py:39). After the plan's change the plan_review module remains the single definition site for the SHARED plan↔code-review constant, but the other two unrelated modules still define their own same-named constants. The proposed source-scan oracle would always fail.
findings.location: AC2, oracle clause (b): 'asserts there is exactly ONE RETAIN_PER_TICKET = assignment in src/rebar/'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC2 oracle (b) to: '(b) asserts that `code_review/sidecar.py` contains no `RETAIN_PER_TICKET =` assignment (i.e., a grep of that file finds zero definition sites, confirming code-review imports rather than redefines the constant)' — this is both accurate and sufficient to enforce the one-definition-site invariant for the plan↔code-review pair without tripping over unrelated modules.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC2(b)'s source-scan oracle to account for the two pre-existing `RETAIN_PER_TICKET =` assignments in `completion_sidecar.py` (=10) and `overlap/digest_sidecar.py` (=1) — scope the scan to only `plan_review/sidecar.py` and `code_review/sidecar.py`, or change the assertion to 'exactly one definition of `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50`', so the test passes after the plan's changes are applied.
findings.finding: AC2(b)'s oracle test asserts 'exactly ONE RETAIN_PER_TICKET = assignment in src/rebar/', but two other files already have independent RETAIN_PER_TICKET = assignments — src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py:34 (=10) and src/rebar/llm/overlap/digest_sidecar.py:39 (=1) — and the plan does not modify or acknowledge them. The proposed test will fail even after the plan's changes are correctly applied.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2(b)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC2(b) scan to: assert that the count of `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` (the new value) in `src/rebar/` is exactly 1, OR assert that `grep -r 'RETAIN_PER_TICKET =' src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ src/rebar/llm/code_review/` finds exactly one result (the definition in plan_review/sidecar.py, with code_review importing it). Add a note that completion_sidecar.py and overlap/digest_sidecar.py are intentionally independent constants.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how the code-review prune distinguishes its own '-REVIEW_RESULT.json' files from plan-review '-REVIEW_RESULT.json' files (same EVENT_TYPE, same filename suffix, same ticket directory), and add a payload-schema-based filter (e.g. open each file and check `schema == 'code_review_result_v1'`) or a distinct filename discriminator to prevent cross-sidecar deletion.
findings.finding: Both plan_review and code_review sidecars share EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT', so both write files ending in '-REVIEW_RESULT.json' to the same ticket directory. The proposed code_review prune filters on 'this module's own EVENT_TYPE' (i.e., the filename suffix '-REVIEW_RESULT.json'), which is identical to the plan_review suffix. On a ticket that receives both plan-review and code-review events, the code-review prune will count and potentially delete plan-review sidecar files (and vice versa), because the two event types are indistinguishable by filename alone.
findings.location: Scope section — code_review/sidecar.py prune spec: 'filter on this module's own EVENT_TYPE'; also src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py line 21 (EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT') and src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py line 28 (EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a schema-based filter in the prune: after listing `-REVIEW_RESULT.json` files, open each and check `event['data']['schema']`, only marking `code_review_result_v1` files as candidates for pruning. This mirrors the schema-guard already present in `_latest_payload_with_ts`. Alternatively, consider using a distinct EVENT_TYPE (e.g. `CODE_REVIEW_RESULT`) for code-review sidecars, though that is a larger change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC2(d)'s monkeypatch oracle to account for Python's frozen default-argument semantics — either test object identity at import time (AC2(a)/(c) already cover this), or specify patching `prune.__kwdefaults__['keep']` on both functions, or have both prune bodies read `keep` from the live module constant rather than the default parameter.
findings.finding: AC2(d) specifies a monkeypatch oracle — 'monkeypatches the single constant to a small N and asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N (proving both paths read the one constant at prune time, not a captured copy)' — but Python default parameter values are evaluated at function-definition time and frozen into the function object. After `def prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, ...)` is compiled, monkeypatching the module-level `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` name has no effect on the already-captured default. The test as written will fail for both the plan-review and code-review prune functions regardless of correctness, or will pass only for the wrong reason (e.g. if the test calls `prune(ticket_id)` without monkeypatching the function's `__defaults__`). This makes AC2(d) a broken oracle that will mislead implementers.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2(d): 'monkeypatches the single constant to a small N and asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC2(d)'s monkeypatch approach with: have both `prune` bodies resolve the effective keep value as `keep if keep is not RETAIN_PER_TICKET else <module>.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (i.e., read from the live name), OR specify the test as patching `prune.__kwdefaults__` directly. The simplest fix is to drop AC2(d) and rely on AC2(a)/(b)/(c) for the one-constant guarantee, since those already structurally verify it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC2(d)'s test oracle to account for Python default-argument capture semantics: monkeypatching the module-level `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` name after import does NOT change an already-captured function default — instead, either (a) call `prune(..., keep=N)` explicitly in the test (bypassing the default entirely) to verify both functions respect the `keep` parameter, or (b) drop AC2(d) as redundant given AC2(a) and AC2(b) already enforce the single-constant invariant at the structural level.
findings.finding: AC2(d)'s monkeypatch test oracle relies on an unverified (and likely incorrect) assumption about Python default-parameter capture: monkeypatching a module-level constant does NOT retroactively change an already-captured function default, so the proposed test would not prove 'both prune paths read the one constant at prune time.'
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2(d): 'monkeypatches the single constant to a small N and asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N (proving both paths read the one constant at prune time, not a captured copy)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC2(d) with: 'A test that calls both prune functions with an explicit `keep=N` (small N) on a ticket with >N synthetic events and asserts each retains exactly N, verifying both functions honor the parameter — structural single-definition is already enforced by AC2(a)+(b).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC2(b)'s oracle: scope the 'exactly one definition site' scan to the two plan-review/code-review sidecar files specifically (or count the expected total of 3 across src/rebar/ rather than 1), since completion_sidecar.py and overlap/digest_sidecar.py independently define their own RETAIN_PER_TICKET constants.
findings.finding: The AC2(b) oracle asserts 'exactly ONE RETAIN_PER_TICKET = assignment in src/rebar/' but the codebase already contains THREE such definitions (completion_sidecar.py:34, plan_review/sidecar.py:43, overlap/digest_sidecar.py:39). After this story lands, there will still be three. The oracle will fail unconditionally unless scoped to only the two directly-involved files.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2, oracle clause (b): 'asserts there is exactly ONE RETAIN_PER_TICKET = assignment in src/rebar/'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the oracle to: 'a source scan of src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py and src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py finds exactly ONE RETAIN_PER_TICKET = assignment (in plan_review/sidecar.py); code_review/sidecar.py has none (it imports).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC2(d)'s oracle to account for Python default-argument capture: either (a) test via explicit `keep=` override (call `prune(..., keep=small_N)` and assert retention, which is already exercised by AC1), or (b) assert `code_review.sidecar.prune.__kwdefaults__['keep'] is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` to check the function's actual default binding.
findings.finding: The AC2(d) monkeypatch oracle is structurally broken: Python default argument values are captured at function definition time, so `monkeypatch.setattr(plan_review.sidecar, 'RETAIN_PER_TICKET', small_N)` after import will NOT change the already-bound default in `prune(..., keep: int = RETAIN_PER_TICKET, ...)`. The oracle will silently pass regardless of whether the prune functions actually read the live constant.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2, oracle clause (d): 'monkeypatches the single constant to a small N and asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the monkeypatch oracle with: `assert code_review.sidecar.prune.__kwdefaults__['keep'] is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` — this directly inspects the function's bound default and confirms object identity to the imported constant, which actually fails if code_review defines its own literal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC2(d)'s oracle: instead of monkeypatching after import, call both `prune` functions explicitly passing `keep=small_N` as a keyword argument (bypassing the default) and assert N events remain — this directly tests the prune logic respects `keep`; rely on AC2(b)'s source-scan to enforce the single-definition-site invariant (behavioral + structural tests covering orthogonal concerns).
findings.finding: AC2(d)'s monkeypatch approach cannot work as described: `from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar import RETAIN_PER_TICKET` binds the name `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` in `code_review.sidecar`'s module namespace at import time, and both `prune` functions capture the integer as a default parameter value at function-definition time. Patching `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` after import does not change either module's local binding or the already-captured default, so the test 'asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N' will either always pass (because patching has no effect and the default stays 50, which may equal the small N used in the test by coincidence) or always fail — it does not verify what it claims.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2(d): 'monkeypatches the single constant to a small N and asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N (proving both paths read the one constant at prune time, not a captured copy)'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC2(d) as: 'call `plan_review.sidecar.prune(ticket_id, keep=3)` and `code_review.sidecar.prune(ticket_id, keep=3)` explicitly with a small `keep` value and assert exactly 3 events remain on each path — this validates the prune logic without relying on monkeypatching, which cannot affect already-captured default values.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Narrow the AC2 source-scan oracle so it tests the right invariant: instead of 'exactly one `RETAIN_PER_TICKET =` in `src/rebar/`' (false — completion_sidecar.py:34 and overlap/digest_sidecar.py:39 are independent, unrelated constants), assert 'no `RETAIN_PER_TICKET =` assignment exists in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`' (the actual constraint the import-identity design enforces).
findings.finding: AC2's source-scan oracle asserts 'exactly ONE `RETAIN_PER_TICKET =` assignment in `src/rebar/`', but the codebase already has three independent definitions (plan_review/sidecar.py:43, completion_sidecar.py:34, overlap/digest_sidecar.py:39) and the story does not consolidate them — so the oracle test will fail even after this story lands correctly.
findings.location: AC2 — 'asserts there is exactly ONE `RETAIN_PER_TICKET =` assignment in `src/rebar/`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC2 oracle clause 'asserts there is exactly ONE `RETAIN_PER_TICKET =` assignment in `src/rebar/`' with 'asserts there is NO `RETAIN_PER_TICKET =` assignment in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`' — which is the precise invariant the import-identity approach enforces, and which is both passable and meaningful.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace AC2(a)'s `is` identity check (unreliable for small integers due to CPython interning) with a more robust oracle: assert that `code_review.sidecar` has no `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` name in its own `__dict__` or `vars()` (i.e., the name is not defined locally in that module — only imported), which correctly distinguishes import from copy regardless of value.
findings.finding: AC2(a)'s `is` identity check on integer 50 is a tautology in CPython due to small-integer interning: `50 is 50` evaluates True even when two separate `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` literals are assigned in different modules, because CPython interns integers in [-5, 256]. The test passes even if code_review defines its own duplicate literal — defeating the entire purpose of the check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2(a): '`code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (OBJECT IDENTITY, so a copied literal that merely equals 50 fails)'
findings.suggested_fix: Assert `'RETAIN_PER_TICKET' not in vars(code_review.sidecar) or code_review.sidecar.__dict__.get('RETAIN_PER_TICKET') is not defined at module level` — concretely: use `inspect.getmodule` or check that `code_review.sidecar.__dict__` does NOT contain `'RETAIN_PER_TICKET'` as a key (it would only appear there if locally assigned; an import via `from X import Y` creates a local name that DOES appear in `__dict__`, but AC2(b)'s source scan is the stronger gate — consider making AC2(b) the primary oracle for this invariant and dropping AC2(a)'s `is` check entirely).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Note in AC2(c) that the `is` identity check is vacuously true for integer 50 due to CPython interning, and that AC2(b)'s source scan is the operative guard; optionally strengthen AC2(c) by checking `code_review.sidecar.prune.__kwdefaults__['keep'] is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` after ensuring the prune signature uses a call-time lookup (per the fix for AC2(d)) rather than a captured default.
findings.finding: AC2(c)'s object-identity check (`is`) on the `keep` default parameter is unreliable for the integer 50: CPython interns small integers (−5 to 256), so `50 is 50` is always True even when code_review defines its own literal `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` rather than importing — the oracle cannot distinguish import from copy for this specific value.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2, sub-clause (c)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or clarify AC2(c): the function-default object-identity check for integer values ≤ 256 is not a meaningful discriminator from AC2(a); if retained, note that it is only meaningful in combination with AC2(a) (which actually fails on a copied literal).
findings.finding: The AC2(c) object-identity assertion ('asserts both prune functions' keep default parameter is that same object') cannot distinguish an imported constant from a copied `50` literal because CPython interns small integers: `50 is 50` is True for two independent literals in the same process, so the identity check passes even if code_review/sidecar.py writes `keep: int = 50` rather than importing.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2, oracle clause (c): 'asserts both prune functions' keep default parameter is that same object'

findings.finding: AC2's stated oracle ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') is weaker than AC2's stated criterion ('The cap is one named constant governing both paths'). A mirrored-constants implementation (two separate variables both set to 50) satisfies the oracle but violates the 'one named constant' structural claim, meaning the AC can be green while the criterion is false.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'The cap is one named constant governing both plan- and code-review prune paths. Oracle: a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value.'
findings.suggested_fix: AC2 oracle: 'a test asserting `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (import identity), confirming a single definition site; plus `grep -c "RETAIN_PER_TICKET\s*=" src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` should equal 1 (only one assignment exists in the codebase).'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a `logger.warning(...)` (or equivalent structured log) inside the exception handler in the new code-review `prune()` so that swallowed failures are visible in logs, matching the observability bar the plan sets for the feature (bounded retention).
findings.finding: Prune failures are silently swallowed with no log, metric, or trace emitted — if git rm or git commit fails repeatedly (e.g., due to permissions, lock contention, or misconfiguration), code-review sidecars will grow unbounded again with no operator-visible signal.
findings.location: Scope section — code_review/sidecar.py prune spec: 'best-effort and exception-swallowing — a failed prune never fails the gate'
findings.suggested_fix: In the except block of code_review prune(), add: `logger.warning('code_review sidecar prune failed for ticket %s (suppressed): %s', ticket_id, exc)` — cheap, zero-failure-risk, and makes silent failures detectable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the out-of-scope code_review/sidecar.py deliverable: either (a) update parent db7b to explicitly expand scope to include code-review retention (which stales db7b's completion attestation and requires its re-review), OR (b) split the code-review prune addition into a separate leaf under the correct parent (e.g., an epic b744/WS4 story), with this leaf covering only the plan_review RETAIN_PER_TICKET raise.
findings.finding: The leaf's second deliverable — adding prune() and RETAIN_PER_TICKET retention to src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py — is entirely outside parent db7b's declared scope. Parent db7b's What, Scope, and all ACs reference only `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.prune`; code_review/sidecar.py belongs to epic b744/WS4 (a wholly different parent lineage). The parent wins: the leaf may not deliver something the parent's plan does not contain without first updating the parent.
findings.location: Leaf §Scope (code_review/sidecar.py bullets) and §Current state; parent db7b §Scope ('rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.prune'), §What, and AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt option (b): scope this leaf to plan_review/sidecar.py only (raise RETAIN_PER_TICKET 10→50, consistent with parent db7b AC4). Create a sibling leaf under epic b744/WS4 for the code_review/sidecar.py prune addition, with its own parent contract. The two leaves can share the constant via import as specified, but each must sit under the parent that owns its primary file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback data-state assertion to the Compatibility & Rollback section: explicitly state that code-review REVIEW_RESULT events pruned on the first post-deploy emit are permanently removed from the tickets branch HEAD (recoverable only via git history), and that rollback restores the code behavior but not the pruned event files.
findings.finding: The Compatibility & Rollback section describes rollback options ('sets the constant back to 10' or 'removes the code-review prune') but does NOT state the data state after rollback: code-review REVIEW_RESULT events that were git-rm'd and committed on the first emit after deployment are permanently absent from the tickets branch HEAD. T11 requires the plan to assert the schema/data STATE after rollback, not merely that a rollback operation can be performed.
findings.location: Compatibility & Rollback section
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the Compatibility & Rollback section: 'Post-rollback data state: code-review REVIEW_RESULT events deleted by the first post-deploy prune are permanently removed from the tickets branch HEAD; they remain in git object history and can be recovered via `git checkout <pre-prune-sha> -- <path>` if needed. Rollback restores the pruning behavior (unbounded growth resumes) but does NOT restore already-pruned event files. This is acceptable because the events are audit logs, not application state, and git history provides a recovery path.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts there is an existing 'code-review prune path' in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` that needs only a constant update, but the file contains no `prune()` function and no `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant — the prune path must be written from scratch and wired into `emit()`.
findings.location: Scope section: `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` — 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune (shared or mirrored constant)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Note: `code_review/sidecar.py` has no existing prune path — this change must add a `prune()` function (mirroring plan_review/sidecar.prune) and call it from `emit()` after `append_event`, in addition to declaring the shared constant.'

findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py contains no prune() function and no RETAIN_PER_TICKET constant — the 'code-review prune path' the plan names as an existing edit target does not exist; wiring it in requires creating both the function and a call from emit(), which is new behavior (CREATION), not an update.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py / AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Scope entry for code_review/sidecar.py to read: 'Add RETAIN_PER_TICKET (shared with or mirrored from plan_review/sidecar.py) and a prune() function modelled on plan_review/sidecar.prune(); wire it as a best-effort call in emit() after append_event — mirroring the plan-review emit() pattern.'

findings.finding: The code_review/sidecar.py file has no prune() function and its emit() makes no prune call — there is no 'code-review prune path' to govern with a shared or mirrored constant. The plan's mechanism for the code-review half (add/share the constant) is incomplete: a constant with no calling code does not bound code-review sidecar growth.
findings.location: What / Scope section — 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py — ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune'
findings.suggested_fix: In code_review/sidecar.py: (1) define RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50 (or import it from plan_review.sidecar); (2) add a prune() function identical in structure to plan_review/sidecar.prune() filtering on f"-{EVENT_TYPE}.json"; (3) call prune(target_ticket, repo_root=repo_root) at the end of emit() (best-effort, exception-swallowed), mirroring line 63 of plan_review/sidecar.py.

findings.finding: The code-review sidecar (`src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`) has no `prune` function and no `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant — confirmed by tool search. The plan's Scope says 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune' and AC2 oracles 'a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value,' treating a prune path as already existing. It does not exist. The plan therefore fails to specify: (1) that a new `prune()` function must be authored for this file, (2) where in `emit()` it should be called, and (3) whether it should follow the same best-effort/exception-swallowing convention as the plan-review counterpart.
findings.location: Scope section / `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` / AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet for `code_review/sidecar.py`: 'Add a `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, repo_root=None)` function mirroring `plan_review/sidecar.prune` (same git-rm logic, same best-effort/exception-swallowing pattern) and call it from `emit()` immediately after the `append_event` call, returning `True` on success regardless of prune outcome.'

findings.finding: The plan's Scope and AC2 claim a 'code-review prune path' exists in `code_review/sidecar.py` and needs its constant aligned — but that file has neither a `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant nor a `prune()` function. Proposing a 'shared constant governs both prune paths' when one path doesn't yet exist is premature abstraction; AC2's oracle ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') would require adding new production code that the plan never acknowledges as a new addition.
findings.location: Scope section ('src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py — ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune') and AC2 ('The cap is one named constant governing both plan- and code-review prune paths')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune (shared or mirrored constant)' with an explicit statement: 'Add a `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` constant and a `prune()` call in `code_review/sidecar.py`'s `emit()`, mirroring the plan-review sidecar pattern.' Then update AC2's oracle to describe the new test accordingly. Alternatively, if code-review growth is not a current concern, remove the code-review prune from scope entirely and narrow AC2 to plan-review only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that if the `git commit` step fails after `git rm`, the prune implementation should attempt `git restore --staged <files>` (or equivalent) to un-stage the deletions before swallowing the exception, preventing partial-deletion state from persisting across invocations.
findings.finding: If `git rm` succeeds in staging file deletions but the subsequent `git commit` fails, the event files are removed from the working tree but the deletion is not recorded in git history — future prune invocations see a smaller file set and may never re-commit the staged deletions, creating a silent audit-trail gap where events are unrecoverable but also not durably pruned.
findings.location: Scope section — code_review/sidecar.py prune spec: 'git rm all but the newest keep, commit with --no-verify'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the git rm + commit sequence so that on commit failure the code runs `subprocess.run(['git', 'restore', '--staged', '.'], ...)` within the same exception handler before suppressing the error, restoring the index to pre-prune state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the concurrent-emit TOCTOU race in the plan (or specify a serialization mechanism such as a per-ticket file lock or git-level retry) so the shared mutable state on the tickets branch is accessed with defined concurrency semantics.
findings.finding: The list-then-delete sequence in `prune()` is not atomic: two concurrent `emit()` calls on the same ticket both list the same files, both select the same 'oldest' files for deletion, and then race on `git rm` and `git commit` — the second commit will fail (or silently conflict), and with exception-swallowing the race outcome is unpredictable, potentially leaving more than 50 events or silently losing events.
findings.location: Scope section — code_review/sidecar.py prune spec; also applies to plan_review/sidecar.py by extension

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
